topic: The Early Church: The Church in Acts
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Did Paul write the Acts of the Apostles?
items.correct: No - Acts is traditionally attributed to Luke, the author of the third Gospel, as the second volume of his work addressed to Theophilus.
items.misconception: Paul wrote Acts because he is its main character.
items.random_wrong: Acts was written by Peter as his memoirs.
items.why: Students infer authorship from the central character; the Luke-Acts connection is fundamental to studying the book's purpose.

items.q: Was the early Church free of disagreement?
items.correct: No - Acts records real disputes: the complaint of the Hellenist widows in Acts 6, the circumcision controversy settled at Jerusalem in Acts 15, and the split between Paul and Barnabas.
items.misconception: The first Christians always agreed and never argued.
items.random_wrong: The early Church had no leaders, so there was nothing to disagree about.
items.why: An idealised picture obscures how Acts presents conflict being resolved, which is exactly what exam questions probe.

items.q: At Pentecost, did the disciples speak meaningless ecstatic sounds?
items.correct: Acts 2 describes them speaking in other real languages that visiting pilgrims understood, each hearing in their own tongue.
items.misconception: Pentecost was an outburst of unintelligible babbling that no one could understand.
items.random_wrong: At Pentecost the disciples were given the gift of prophecy about Rome's fall.
items.why: Students conflate Acts 2 with later debates about glossolalia; Luke stresses comprehension as a sign of the gospel's universal reach.

items.q: What happened at Pentecost in Acts 2?
items.correct: The Holy Spirit came upon the disciples, Peter preached, and about three thousand people were baptised
items.random_wrong: Saul was converted on the Damascus road
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Holy Spirit came upon the disciples, Peter preached, and about three thousand people were baptised. “Jesus ascended into heaven” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who was the first Christian martyr?
items.why: It's Stephen. “Peter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did the Council of Jerusalem in Acts 15 decide?
items.correct: Gentile converts did not need to be circumcised or keep the whole Jewish Law
items.misconception: The Church should remain entirely Jewish
items.random_wrong: Paul should stop his missionary journeys
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gentile converts did not need to be circumcised or keep the whole Jewish Law. “The Church should remain entirely Jewish” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What happened to Saul on the road to Damascus?
items.correct: He was blinded by a light, heard the voice of Jesus and was converted
items.misconception: He was arrested by Roman soldiers
items.random_wrong: He met Peter for the first time
items.why: The answer examiners want is: He was blinded by a light, heard the voice of Jesus and was converted. “He was arrested by Roman soldiers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: According to Acts 2:41, about how many people were baptised at Pentecost?
items.why: Exact answer: 3000 people. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
